threats.title: Stored XSS via bypassSecurityTrustHtml in admin panel — administration.component.ts
threats.description: The administration component renders user emails and feedback comments using DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(), which disables Angular's built-in XSS protection. An attacker who registers with an email containing a script tag or injects malicious HTML via feedback can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of any admin who views the administration panel.
threats.controls_in_place: Angular DomSanitizer present but explicitly bypassed

mitigation.title: Remove bypassSecurityTrustHtml calls; use Angular binding safely
steps: Use [innerText] binding for email display instead of [innerHTML]
For HTML-containing comments, use a sanitize pipe instead of bypass

threats.title: JWT stored in localStorage — XSS-accessible token storage — login.component.ts
threats.description: The JWT authentication token is stored in localStorage, which is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page. A successful XSS attack can exfiltrate the token via `localStorage.getItem('token')`, allowing session hijacking. HttpOnly cookies would prevent JavaScript access.

mitigation.title: Migrate JWT storage to HttpOnly cookie
steps: Configure backend to set JWT as HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie
Update frontend to remove localStorage usage for token
Remove cookie-parser's plaintext cookie handling for token

threats.title: Client-side only admin guard — Angular route guard bypass — app.guard.ts
threats.description: AdminGuard and AccountingGuard are client-side only Angular route guards. They can be bypassed by directly navigating to /administration or /accounting with a non-admin JWT. The backend has no matching server-side authorization check that restricts the admin UI's data endpoints to admin roles.

mitigation.title: Implement server-side admin role enforcement
steps: Create security.isAdmin() middleware checking JWT role === 'admin'
Apply to all admin-specific REST endpoints (user management, order history, etc.)
Do not rely on client-side guards for security enforcement

threats.title: DOM XSS via search result rendering — search-result.component.ts
threats.description: Search results are rendered using bypassSecurityTrustHtml, allowing stored XSS payloads injected via product names/descriptions (which are updatable by any authenticated user due to PUT /api/Products/:id being unprotected) to execute in the browser of any user who views search results.

mitigation.title: Use safe Angular bindings for search result rendering
steps: Use Angular's built-in [innerText] binding for product names/descriptions

threats.title: OAuth token exposed in URL fragment — oauth/oauth.component.ts
threats.description: The OAuth component processes the access token from the URL fragment (window.location.href). URL fragments containing tokens can be leaked via Referer headers, browser history, and server logs. This is the implicit OAuth flow which is deprecated due to token leakage risks.

mitigation.title: Migrate from OAuth implicit flow to authorization code flow with PKCE
steps: Implement PKCE flow instead of implicit flow
Never pass tokens in URL fragments
Use state parameter to prevent CSRF
